div.p: It can be seen that, J 2 , and J 3 are flavor-dependent Hund's, similar to J , which merely exchange spins but do not violate any flavor charge conservation, while V 2 and K 2 are pair-scatterings that violate the orbital charge conservation. V 2 is spinindependent, and K 2 exchanges spins in the meanwhile. In particular, the bare interaction obeys parametrization of Eq. (C1) as well. The corresponding values (without tildes) can be easily read off by comparing Table II to the bare levels solved in Appendix A 4,
div.formula: U 1 = U + J вҖІ H , J = J A -J вҖІ H , (C2) U 2 = U + J H , J 2 = J A -J H , V 2 = J вҖІ H , K 2 = J A -J вҖІ H (C3) U 3 = U + J вҖІ H , J 3 = -J вҖІ H (C4) U 4 = U + 2J H (C5)
div.p: It can also be seen that, V 2 and K 2 correspond to the last and second-last terms in Eq. (A32).

Next, we need to express the susceptibilities of all the charge-0 operators through the zero-frequency vertex function О“ 0 , using Eq. (B42). If any susceptibility is asymptotically frozen in the Kondo regime, then the asymptotic behavior of О“ 0 will be restricted. The charge O 000 , valley charge O 0z0 , and the total spin O 00z are still conserved, hence by virtue of the Ward identities, Eq. (B42) provides an exact relation between the exact charge ПҮ c , valley ПҮ v and spin ПҮ s susceptibilities and the exact vertex О“ 0 . When the ground states are dominated by the [E 2 , 0] states, ПҮ c,v,s still freeze as in the flattened case, giving rise to 3 exact equations constraining the 9 unknown parameters,
div.formula: 0 = ПҖ вҲҶ 0 sin 2 Оҙ f ПҮ c = 8 1 - sin 2 Оҙ f ПҖ вҲҶ 0 Оә[O 000 , О“ 0 ] (C6) 0 = ПҖ вҲҶ 0 sin 2 Оҙ f ПҮ v = 8 1 - sin 2 Оҙ f ПҖ вҲҶ 0 Оә[O 0z0 , О“ 0 ] (C7) 0 = ПҖ вҲҶ 0 sin 2 Оҙ f ПҮ s = 8 1 - sin 2 Оҙ f ПҖ вҲҶ 0 Оә[O 00z , О“ 0 ] .(C8)
div.p: The Оә functions are defined by Eq. (B39), and will be evaluated in Eq. (C11). The 'orbital charge' O z00 , on the other hand, no longer remains conserved, thus Eq. (B42) only estimates the corresponding quasi-particle susceptibility ПҮ o . As the exact susceptibility ПҮ o will exactly equal to ПҮ o when the orbital U(1) is recovered, we argue that Eq. (B42) still approximates ПҮ o if the orbital U(1) is weakly broken in the effective theory. In the following, we show the self-consistency of this approximation by checking that the renormalized interaction indeed approximately respects the higher U(1) Г—4 Г—SU(2) Г—2 symmetry. Since the [E 2 , 0] states still hold as eigenstates of zero eigenvalue for the O z00 operator, ПҮ o should be asymptotically frozen as well. This contributes the fourth constraint, albeit with approximation,
div.formula: 0 = ПҖ вҲҶ 0 sin 2 Оҙ f ПҮ o вүҲ 8 1 - sin 2 Оҙ f ПҖ вҲҶ 0 Оә[O z00 , О“ 0 ] (C9)
div.p: Crucially, we note that, in the flattened case, the operators O zzz and O 00z have been shown as linked by a symmetry action in the large U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 group (Appendix B 3), therefore, for the [E 2 , 0] ground states, the two susceptibilities, ПҮ zzz and ПҮ s , are frozen simultaneously. Physically, this can be understood as follows: O 00z ВұO zzz 2 probes the spin susceptibility of each angular momentum flavor, while for both states in the [E 2 , 0] irrep, each angular momentum flavor either is empty, or owns a spin singlet, hence neither flavor can exhibit a net spin. With the symmetry broken to U(1) Г—3 Г— SU(2), 1) O zzz no longer remains conserved, hence we can only approximate ПҮ zzz вүҲ ПҮ zzz with ПҮ zzz expressed through Eq. (B42), and 2) ПҮ zzz should rely on the new 4 parameters of О“ 0 in a different way from ПҮ s . However, as long as the same [E 2 , 0] ground states dominate in the heavy Fermi liquid, ПҮ zzz will be frozen as well as ПҮ s , and by its different reliance on О“ 0 , this imposes a fifth constraint,
div.formula: 0 = ПҖ вҲҶ 0 sin 2 Оҙ f ПҮ zzz вүҲ 8 1 - sin 2 Оҙ f ПҖ вҲҶ 0 Оә[O zzz , О“ 0 ] . (C10)
div.p: The five Оә functions are evaluated according to Eq. (B39) as follows,
div.formula: пЈ« пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈӯ Оә[O 000 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O 00z , О“ 0 ] Оә[O 0z0 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O z00 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O zzz , О“ 0 ]
div.p: six out of which arise from spin-0 operators,
div.formula: пЈ« пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈӯ Оә[O 0x0 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O x00 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O y00 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O xx0 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O yx0 , О“ 0 ] Оә[O zz0 , О“ 0 ] пЈ¶ пЈ· пЈ· пЈ· пЈ· пЈ· пЈ· пЈ· пЈё = пЈ« пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈ¬ пЈӯ

Comparison of the renormalized two-particle scattering channels that are obtained for the flattened interaction (with U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry) and the original interaction (with U(1) Г—3 Г— SU(2) symmetry). The limit вҲҶ0 вүӘ JA,H is assumed. The five dashed rectangles indicate the estimated energy ranges for the five independent channels of the flattened interaction, namely U1 -J , U1 + J , and U2,3,4, respectively, and the nine colored boxes indicate the estimated energy ranges for the nine independent channels of the original interaction.

div.p: E. Quasi-particle mean-field theory of the superconductivity

div.head: Effective BdG Hamiltonian
div.p: The free part of topological heavy fermion model is given by [59] ). However, c-electron is described by a continuous field that cannot be regularized to a lattice due to the symmetry anomaly [75]. Thus, momentum k of c-electrons is within a cutoff Оӣ c that will be sent to infinity in the end [59]. The matrices H (c,О·) and H (cf,О·) are defined by
div.formula: H (c,О·) (k) = 0 2Г—2 v вӢҶ (О·k x Пғ 0 + ik y Пғ z ) v вӢҶ (О·k x Пғ 0 -ik y Пғ z ) M Пғ x , H (cf,О·) (k) = ОіПғ 0 + v вҖІ вӢҶ (О·k x Пғ x + k y Пғ y ) 0 2Г—2 . (E2)
div.p: The parameter О» is the spread of the Wannier functions of f -electrons, and it suppresses the hybridization at large momentum. In this work we adopt the parameters: Оі = -24.75meV, v вӢҶ = -4.303eV вҖў Г…, v вҖІ вӢҶ = 1.623eV вҖў Г…, О» = 1.4131/k Оё , M = 3.697meV. The resulting band structure is shown in the inset of Fig. 1

div.head: (a).
div.p: The free Hamiltonian has time-reversal T and D 6 point group, generated by C 2z -rotation, C 3z -rotation, and C 2x -rotation symmetries. Their representations on f -electrons and c-electrons are given by (Eq. (A1))
div.formula: T = Пғ 0 П„ x ПӮ 0 K, C 2z = Пғ x П„ x ПӮ 0 , C 3z = e i 2ПҖ 3 Пғ z П„ z ПӮ 0 , C 2x = Пғ x П„ 0 ПӮ 0 ,(E3)
div.p: and T = (Пғ 0 П„ x ПӮ 0 вҠ• Пғ 0 П„ x ПӮ 0 )K, C 2z = Пғ x П„ x ПӮ 0 вҠ• Пғ x П„ x ПӮ 0 , C 3z = e i 2ПҖ 3 Пғ z П„ z ПӮ 0 вҠ• Пғ 0 П„ 0 ПӮ 0 , C 2x = Пғ x П„ 0 ПӮ 0 вҠ• Пғ x П„ 0 ПӮ 0 , (E4) respectively. Detailed derivations of these symmetries are given sections S2A and S2B of the supplementary material Ref. [59]. Readers may directly verify that Eq. (E1) is invariant under these symmetries. In this work we do not distinguish the singleparticle and the second-quantized representations of symmetry operators. The correspondence between the two representations are given after Eq. (A1).

Eq. (E1) with Coulomb interaction is solved within the framework of dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) in Refs. [62,65,66]. Here we are only interested in the doped correlated insulator at the filling ОҪ = -2 -ОҙОҪ, where the вҲҶ 0 вүӘ J is assumed. In the mean-field calculations, we will regard z as an additional input to ОҙОҪ. In the вҲҶ 0 вүӘ J limit, the f -filling is frozen to ОҪ f = -2, indicating that the doping is fully undertaken by c-electrons, i.e., ОҪ c = ОҪ -ОҪ f = -ОҙОҪ.

To describe the quasi-particles at ОҪ = -2 -ОҙОҪ, we express f -electron operators in terms of the quasi-particles operators as f RОұО·s = z 1 2 f RОұО·s . We also introduce the DMFT self-consistent on-site energies Пө f , Пө c1 = Пө c2 , Пө c3 = Пө c4 for f -electrons, a = 1, 2 c-electrons, a = 3, 4 c-electrons, respectively [62]. Then the effective free Hamiltonian for the quasi-particle can be written as One can see that the cf hybridization is suppressed by z 1 2 . Пө f , Пө c,1 , Пө c,3 should be determined self-consistently in the DMFT plus Hartree-Fock calculations. Here we provide a quick approach to estimate Пө f , Пө c,1 , Пө c,3 for given z and ОҪ = -2 -ОҙОҪ. First, it is found that G = Пө c,3 -Пө c,1 is insensitive to doping and can be approximated as a fixed quantity (taken as 8meV here) [62]. We then search for Пө f and Пө c,1 that give the correct fillings ОҪ f = -2 and ОҪ c = -ОҙОҪ.

In DMFT, the two-particle irreducible vertex in the lattice is purely local and equals the two-particle irreducible vertex of the impurity model, for which the susceptibility in the lattice model can be evaluated as ПҮ -1 q = ПҮ 0 q -1 + О“ loc , О“ loc = ПҮ -1 locПҮ 0 loc -1 , where ПҮ q (ПҮ 0 q ) is the momentum dependent interacting (free) susceptibility in the lattice model, ПҮ loc (ПҮ 0 loc ) is the susceptibility in the interacting (free) single impurity model, and О“ loc is the two-particle irreducible vertex in the single impurity model [85]. We emphasize that ПҮ loc can not diverge as there is no symmetry breaking in zero dimension, while ПҮ q from the above expression can diverge and lead to symmetry breaking, e.g., the pairing instability. A pairing instability will yield a mean field ground state with non zero pairing order parameter. We take the irreducible vertex z 2 О“ p -multiplied by z 2 -in the pairing channel as the effective interaction in mean-field calculation, where z 2 О“ p are calculated in Appendix B 5. (One would overcount Feynman diagrams if z 2 О“ is used instead [85].) For the sake of investigating superconducting orders, it suffices to keep the strongest channel in О“ p , i.e., the d-wave E 2 states (Eq. (A59)). The effect of other channels will be discussed in the end of this subsection. At ОҪ f = -2, the pairing potential is determined by the renormalized interaction J via (Eq. (B133))

They are coupled to microscopic phonon modes via the dynamical valley Jahn-Teller effect. (b) Energy bands (black) as a result of hybridization between f -bands (orange) and itinerant Dirac c-bands (blue). (c) Heavy Fermi liquid bands at ОҪ = -2.5 with the quasi-particle weight z = 0.3. Orange and blue colors represent contributions from f -and c-electrons, respectively. (d) and (e) sketch the f -occupation, |ОҪ f |, and Kondo temperature, TK, as functions of the total flat band filling |ОҪ| in DMFT calculations in the absence of JA,H, respectively.

Pairing channels of the bare interaction coincide. (b) Occupations of graphene sub-lattices (A, B) and valleys (K, K вҖІ ) of the lowest E2 and A1 states. (c) The flattened interaction. (d) Two-electron ground states in the parameter space of JA and JH.As pairing channels of the bare interaction, the energies are repulsive due to U , hence they cannot form Cooper pairs. (e) The renormalized interaction U1 -J for d-wave pairings as a function of the Kondo energy scale вҲҶ0 вҲј TK, which is assumed to be much smaller than the bare repulsion U . With other parameters fixed, U expoentially suppresses вҲҶ0, hence a smaller вҲҶ0 also implies a larger U .

figure.head: FIG. 4 .

The inset shows the C2zT sewing matrix phase П• k on the Fermi surface. (c) BdG bands of the gapped nematic d-wave SC, where the gap function is still highly anisotropic. The lower panels in (b) and (c) are the corresponding densities of states calculated using a Lorentz spread 0.004meV.

We also thank Chang-Ming Yue, Xiao-Bo Lu, and Seung-Sup Lee for useful discussions. Z.-D. S., Y.-J. W. and G.-D. Here we regard I 1,2 and I 3,4 as the left and right matrix indices respectively. tr[ О“ 0 вҖў О“ 0 вҖ ] equals the sum of squares of the eigenvalues. О“ 0 is a 64-dimensional matrix. Its eigenspace with nonzero eigenvalues consists of the 56 anti-symmetric states. Each of the 28 states discussed in Appendix A 5 appears twice in the eigenspace because states related by permutation have been regarded physically identical. Suppose ОҰ 1 is a two-electron state with the energy E. Define ОҰ 2 as the permutation of ОҰ 1 , it represents the same state. The physical energy is given by the generalized eigenvalue problem defined by the Hamiltonian matrix H nm = вҹЁОҰ n | ДӨ|ОҰ m вҹ© and overlap matrix S nm = вҹЁОҰ n |ОҰ m вҹ©. There must be H = E(Пғ 0 -Пғ x ) and S = Пғ 0 -Пғ x such that the generalized eigenvalue is E. However, the Hamiltonian matrix H itself has two eigenvalues: 2E and 0. Therefore, each of 28 two-electron state contribute to an eigenvalue 2E and an eigenvalue 0. Thereby,

C. Anderson impurity problem with the original interaction

In this section, we apply a phenomenological susceptibility analysis to the original U(1) Г—3 Г— SU(2) interaction in the вҲҶ 0 вүӘ J A,H limit. The procedures parallel Appendix B, albeit due to the lower symmetry, less susceptibilities can be exactly expressed through the Ward identities, and approximations using the quasi-particle susceptibilities have to be adopted. We find the results can recover our main conclusions drawn from the flattened U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry, and in the low-energy end controlled by the same [E 2 , 0] ground states, the U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 -breaking parameters are restricted to a rather narrow range. The first step is to re-parametrize the zero-frequency vertex function (equivalently, all the two-particle scattering channels) in the most general form according to the continuous and discrete symmetries. To make a convenient comparison to the flattened interaction, we start with the general parametrization of the flattened interaction obtained in Appendix A 5 (using U 1,2,3,4 and J ), break the U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry to U(1) Г—3 Г— SU (2), and identify the level splittings. The results are also necessarily a re-parametrization to the two-particle levels obtained for the bare original interaction, which are listed in Appendix A 4. We summarize the results in Table II.

For the two spin singlets (triplets) of energy U 1 -J (U 1 + J ) under the flattened interaction, they already form the 2) group, thus their degeneracy is still protected by the lower symmetry. For the four states of energy U 4 under the flattened interaction, they form a [E 1 + E 2 , 0] representation of D 6 Г— SU (2). Although E 1 + E 2 is reducible under D 6 actions, they are related by a valley U(1) action, hence will be irreducible under the full symmetry group that also includes valley U(1). We keep the corresponding energies parametrized by U 1 вҲ“ J and U 4 as before.

For the eight states of energy U 2 under the flattened interaction, due to the breaking of the two independent SU(2) rotations, they will split to two spin singlets and two spin triplets. The two spin singlets (triplets) possess opposite orbital charges, N o = Вұ2, which is, however, not related by any symmetry. Therefore, splittings within the two singlets and within the two triplets are both expected. We thus need 3 new parameters -J 2 describes the overall spin splitting, V 2 -K 2 describes the splitting within singlets, and V 2 + K 2 describes the splitting within triplets. The two singlet levels
, respectively, and the two triplet levels

The eight states of energy U 3 would split as the above eight states if crystalline symmetries were not considered. However, as the two spin singlets (triplets) possess opposite valley charges, N v = Вұ2, they necessarily stay degenerate since C 2z anticommutes with N v . We thus only need 1 new parameter J 3 to describe the spin splitting, so that singlets (triplets) have energy U 3 -J 3 (U 3 + J 3 ). Written in terms of the representation of D 6 Г— SU(2), the two spin singlets (triplets) form

The wave-functions of each two-particle scattering channel, F вҖ i , can be found in Appendix A 4 in one-to-one correspondence by tracking the [ПҒ, j] labels. With both the energies E i , parametrized in terms of the 9 free parameters, and the wave-functions F вҖ i known, the effective two-particle interaction can be constructed as i E i F вҖ i F i , and the zero-frequency vertex can be obtained accordingly. The results, in terms of the renormalized values (with a tilde), read
Eqs. (C6) to (C10) thus imply
where sin 2 Оҙ f = 1 2 is employed. By summing up Eq. (C13) to Eq. (C16), importantly, we directly arrive at

which immediately recovers our main results obtained using the flattened approximation -one of the two pairing channels with energies U 1 вҲ“ J must be attractive, albeit now with an approximate sign due to the lack of Ward identities. Besides, by comparing Eq. (C13) and Eq. (C16) only, we obtain that
where the first equation reduces the dimension of the 'flattened-symmetry-breaking' parameter space by one, and the second equation again recovers the flattened result. Moreover, we obtain
which differs from the flattened result with only one new parameter J 3 .

As is also exploited in the flattened case, the not-yet-restricted parameters cannot vary unboundedly, otherwise the non-frozen susceptibilities will become negative, leading to an unphysical interactions. This imposes inequality constraints to the parameters in О“ 0 ,

where ПҮ ВөОҪПҒ is still approximated through Eq. (B42), if O ВөОҪПҒ is not conserved. In total, we find thirteen independent inequalities, The constraints from other channels are not independent, as they are related to the above ones by e.g. valley U(1), spin SU(2), and discrete symmetries. Specially, let us observe the last two expressions associated with O xxz and O yxz . By inserting U 1 вүҲ -2ПҖ вҲҶ 0 , ПҮ xxz вүі 0 and ПҮ yxz вүі 0 actually reduce to V 2 вүі 0 and V 2 вүІ 0, respectively, which essentially requires V 2 to vanish. Once again, the dimension of the 'flattened-symmetry-breaking' parameters is further reduced by one.

In addition, the susceptibilities of charge-2 operators (pairing susceptibilities) also ought to be non-negative, which are calculated through Eq. (B101) and are equivalent to finding independent two-electron states. The Оә p functions are given by twoelectron eigen-energies expressed by the renormalized parameters. They contribute nine more independent inequalities. As a result, we are left with five equations and the twenty two inequalities. Altogether, they restrict J to the following range, J вҲҲ [4.6, 10.3] вҲҶ 0 (C25) which again recovers the results of the flattened interaction. Inspired by the well recovery of the flattened results, it is natural to ask, whether the U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry is a good approximation in the Kondo regime when the low energy (local) Hilbert space is nearly restricted to the [E 2 , 0] ground states. For this sake, we investigate the allowed range for the 4 new parameters, J 2,3 , V 2 and K 2 . If they are all forced to vanish, then the above statement holds true. It has already been shown that, by the susceptibility requirements, they are only allowed to vary in a 2-dimensional subspace satisfying J 2 + J 3 вүҲ 0 and V 2 вүҲ 0, hence it suffices to check the allowed range for J2-J3 2 and K 2 . We find,

Their deviation from 0 is considerably smaller than the estimated size for U 1 or U 1 -J . This indeed suggests that the U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry can be deemed as a good approximation for the low-energy physics when the low energy (local) Hilbert space is nearly restricted to the [E 2 , 0] ground states. Finally, in Fig. 6, we compare the estimated energy range for all the renormalized two-particle scattering channels (pairing channels) that are obtained for the flattened interaction (Appendix B 6) in the вҲҶ 0 вүӘ J limit, and for the original interaction in the вҲҶ 0 вүӘ J A,H limit. As has been obtained in Eq. (C17), Eq. (C19) and Eq. (C25), the allowed energy ranges for the U 1 вҲ“ J and U 4 channels are the same for the original interaction and the flattened interaction. For the other channels, breaking the flattened U(1) Г—4 Г— SU(2) Г—2 symmetry to U(1) Г—3 Г— SU(2) only slightly broadens the allowed energy ranges. For either the flattened or the original interaction, in the renormalized vertex, the phenomenological susceptibility analysis finds the d-wave singlets as the lowest pairing channel with attractive strength. We carry out self-consistent Hartree-Fock calculation to study the symmetry-breaking orders stabilized by the flattened interaction at ОҪ = -2. We find the results are consistent with the results obtained using the realistic interaction Hamiltonian detailed Appendix A. Throughout the calculation, we fix U = 58meV, and vary J between 0-4meV. Other parameters are set as the same in Ref. [59]. The results are shown in Fig. 7.

At J = 0, there is only Coulomb interaction but no multiplet splitting interactions. The Hartree-Fock ground state is thus found as Kramers inter-valley coherence (KIVC) state, in accordance with Refs. [22,59]. Over a threshold of J вүҲ 1.2meV, timereversal inter-valley coherent (TIVC) state becomes the ground state, which is consistent with previous mean-field calculations using phonon-mediated interaction [50,51,106], where TIVC is always found beyond a threshold of the K-phonon-mediated interaction. The TIVC order also exhibits the charge KekulГ© pattern as observed in experiments [107].

Here V 1 (> 0), V 2 (> 0) represent the pairing mean-field Hamiltonian terms
respectively. Оё 1,2 are the U(1) phases spontaneously chosen in the symmetry breaking.

-y 2 Вұ id xy orbitals under the symmetry operations and form the twodimensional representation E 2 of D 6 (Eq. (E3)). The linear combination in Eq. (E17) breaks the C 3z symmetry and respects C 2z and time-reversal (T = П„ x K) symmetries. The linear combinations in Eq. (E18) break the time-reversal symmetry and preserve C 6z . Thus, V 1 and V 2 correspond to the nematic d-wave pairing and chiral d-wave pairing, respectively. As we will show in next subsubsection, V 1 leads to a p-wave-like nodal structure in the BdG spectrum due to the Berry's phase on Fermi surface. Now we discuss the omitted higher on-site pairing channels. By referring to Fig. 6, it can be seen that, for the allowed parameter range we obtained, the U 2,3 (U p 2,3 take the same sign) channels always remain repulsive, hence can be directly dropped in the SC mean-fields. Even if they are incorporated, they necessarily lead to vanishing expectation values on these channels. On the other hand, the U 1 + J and U 4 channels (U p 1 + J p and U p 4 take the same sign) can be weakly attractive in a small range. We therefore check with an enlarged the BdG basis, where inter-valley/intra-valley singlets/triplets can be investigated simultaneously, whether the mean-field solution can have an inter-valley triplet or intra-valley singlet component, aside from the leading inter-valley singlet order. For the parameter range J вҲҲ [4.6, 10.3] вҲҶ 0 and вҲҶ 0 вҲҲ [1.7, 7.1]meV adopted in the main-text, we do not observe their formation. The solution is purely the inter-valley d-wave singlet SC.

We also discuss the possible effect of the omitted non-local interactions on the SC order. A non-local interaction either 1) involves the c-electrons, hence involving a mean-field decomposition like вҹЁ f RОұО·s c ka вҖІ О· вҖІ s вҖІ вҹ© or вҹЁc kaО·s c -ka вҖІ О· вҖІ s вҖІ вҹ©, or 2) acts inter-fsite, hence involving a mean-field decomposition вҹЁ f RОұО·s f R вҖІ a вҖІ О· вҖІ s вҖІ вҹ© with R Мё = R вҖІ . As the c-electrons scarcely participate in the heavy flat bands, type 1) should mainly act on remote bands away from the Fermi level, hence can be neglected. Moreover, by discussion in Appendix A 6, the strength of inter-f -site interactions for the quasi-particles should be reduced to вҲј z 2 U inter-site , as a weak repulsive interaction. By raising the energy of inter-site pairings, such interactions cannot blockade the on-site pairings. Therefore, they can be neglected as well.

div.head: The p-wave-like nodal structure in the nematic d-wave phase
div.p: We now show that the V 1 pairing leads to a p-wave-like nodal structure in the BdG spectrum. We consider the weak pairing limit and project Eq. (E12) onto the Fermi surface basis. As shown in Fig. 4(b), there are two Fermi surfaces around the О“ M point, and both of them are dominated by the f -orbitals. For either Fermi surface, we can approximate the Bloch band state around the Fermi surface as
The time-reversal T = П„ x K and C 2z T = Пғ x K symmetries (Eq. ( E3)) indicate
Without loss of generality, we can choose the gauge
According to Ref. [76], П• k must have an odd winding number along the Fermi surface, i.e.,
if the Fermi surface encloses an odd number of Dirac points. In the symmetric Fermi liquid, П• k winding number is Вұ1, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Now we project the V 1 pairing onto the Fermi surface
As П• k winds Вұ2ПҖ along the Fermi surface, there will be two nodes.

The nodes are robust against perturbations that preserve the C 2z T symmetry. For example, we consider a mixture of s-wave pairing and the nematic d-wave pairing. Due to the D 6 point group symmetry, the s-wave pairing must have the form e iОё0 вҖў V 0 Пғ 0 (V 0 > 0). The projected pairing on Fermi surface becomes
The C 2z T symmetry requires Оё 0 = Оё 1 mod ПҖ such that the pairing term gains a uniform phase e -i2Оё0 under C 2z T . There are two pairing nodes on the Fermi surface as long as

The above discussions are based on the weak pairing presumption. If the pairing energy is much larger than the band separation, then the nodes on inner and outer Fermi surfaces will merge each other, leading to a gapped phase.
Refs. [11,13] reported that the d-wave pairing has four nodes on the Fermi surface. The difference is because Refs. [11,13] used the bare band structure of MATBG, where П• k has a winding number 2.

We also discuss the relation of our results to the Euler obstruction [14], which states that a C 2z T -symmetric pairing diagonal in the Chern basis must have zeros in the Brillouin zone if the Euler class [76] of the normal state bands is nontrivial, as it is MATBG. Since f ОұО·s has a large overlap with the C = (-1) Оұ О· Chern basis [59], the nematic d-wave pairing has a large component (more than 95%) in the obstructed channel.
